data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The collapse was an endogenous political revolution where subject populations, exhausted by centuries of extractive tribute obligations and corvée labor required to maintain palatial grandeur, deliberately dismantled the bureaucratic apparatus when central military power waned, rejecting the ideology of kingship itself rather than being conquered by outsiders.", "mechanism": "Internal class conflict and ideological rejection of the palatial social contract leading to voluntary decentralization.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit"], "excludes": ["Egypt", "Sea Peoples as a unified invading army"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Inscriptions or records from the period celebrating the restoration of kingship by the same local populations immediately after the destruction layers.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "An atypical, Marxist-leaning interpretation that reframes the 'Sea Peoples' not as invaders but as a symptom of internal revolt or misidentifies the primary agent of change as the peasantry."}
